questions.question:
Consider the following Pascal function where A and B are non-zero positive integers. What is the value of GET(4, 2)?

function GET(A, B: integer): integer;
begin
  if B = 0 then
    GET := 1
  else if A < B then
    GET := 0
  else
    GET := GET(A-1, B) + GET(A-1, B-1)
end;

A. 4  B. 5  C. 6  D. 7

understand.plain: The function GET(A, B) is a recursive Pascal function. By tracing through the recursion, we can see it computes the binomial coefficient C(A, B) = A! / (B! * (A-B)!). We need to find GET(4, 2) which equals C(4, 2) = 6.

keywords.explain: The number of ways to choose k items from n items, computed as n! / (k! * (n-k)!). Pascal's identity: C(n,k) = C(n-1,k) + C(n-1,k-1).
C(n, k) = C(n-1, k) + C(n-1, k-1) with base cases C(n,0)=1 and C(n,k)=0 when n<k.

given.aim: Find the value of the recursive function GET(4, 2)

terms.meaning: Recursive Pascal function that computes the binomial coefficient C(A, B)
terms.connects: The function we need to evaluate at A=4, B=2

terms.meaning: When B is 0, GET returns 1, corresponding to C(n,0) = 1 for any n
terms.connects: Terminates the recursion when we choose 0 elements

terms.meaning: When A is less than B, GET returns 0, corresponding to C(n,k)=0 when k>n
terms.connects: Terminates the recursion when choosing more items than available
The core recurrence that builds up to the final answer

given.plan: Recognize that GET(A,B) computes C(A,B) (binomial coefficient) by Pascal's identity. Evaluate GET(4,2) = C(4,2) = 4!/(2!*2!) = 6, or trace the recursion tree level by level.

steps.title: Identify the function as the binomial coefficient
steps.apply: The base cases are C(n,0)=1 and C(n,k)=0 for k>n. The recursive case GET(A-1,B)+GET(A-1,B-1) is exactly Pascal's identity C(n,k)=C(n-1,k)+C(n-1,k-1). Therefore GET(A,B) = C(A,B).
steps.note: Recognizing the pattern avoids having to trace the entire recursion tree.

solution.result: GET(4, 2) = 6. The correct answer is C.

questions.formula_note: Pascal's identity for binomial coefficients: C(n,k) = C(n-1,k) + C(n-1,k-1), with base cases C(n,0)=1 and C(n,k)=0 for k>n.

questions.question:
What value would the following function return for the input x = 95?

Function fun(x: integer): integer;
Begin
  If x > 100 then fun := x - 10
  Else fun := fun(fun(x + 11))
End;

A. 89  B. 90  C. 91  D. 92

understand.plain: This is the classic McCarthy 91 function. For any input x <= 100, the function always returns 91. For x > 100, it returns x - 10. Since 95 <= 100, the answer is 91.

keywords.explain: A famous recursive function that returns x-10 if x>100, and 91 for all x<=100. It demonstrates mutual recursion and is used in formal verification.
The function calls itself twice in the else branch: fun(fun(x+11)), where the inner call's result is passed as argument to the outer call.
keywords.example: fun(95) calls fun(fun(106)) = fun(96) which eventually stabilizes to 91

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='500' height='260' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>McCarthy 91 Function: fun(95)</text><rect x='10' y='40' width='480' height='28' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='59' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>fun(95) = fun(fun(106)) since 95 <= 100, x+11=106</text><rect x='10' y='75' width='480' height='28' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='94' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>fun(106) = 106 - 10 = 96 since 106 > 100</text><rect x='10' y='110' width='480' height='28' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='129' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>fun(95) = fun(96) = fun(fun(107))</text><rect x='10' y='145' width='480' height='28' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='164' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>fun(107) = 97 so fun(96) = fun(97) ... continues</text><rect x='10' y='180' width='480' height='28' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='250' y='199' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>For all x <= 100: fun(x) = 91 (McCarthy 91 property)</text><rect x='150' y='218' width='200' height='34' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='250' y='241' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>fun(95) = 91</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Step-by-step trace showing fun(95) calls fun(fun(106)), fun(106)=96, then fun(96) which eventually resolves to 91 due to the McCarthy 91 property.

given.aim: Find the return value of the McCarthy 91 function for input x = 95

terms.meaning: The input value, which is less than or equal to 100
terms.example: Since 95 <= 100, the else branch fires: fun := fun(fun(95+11)) = fun(fun(106))
terms.connects: Triggers the recursive case, not the base case

terms.meaning: When x exceeds 100, the function directly returns x minus 10
terms.connects: The recursion eventually reaches inputs > 100 to terminate

terms.meaning: For all integers x <= 100, fun(x) = 91. This is a proven mathematical property of this exact recurrence.
terms.connects: Directly gives us fun(95) = 91 without full recursion trace

given.plan: Recognize the function as the McCarthy 91 function. Since 95 <= 100, by the McCarthy 91 theorem, fun(95) = 91. Alternatively, trace: fun(95) = fun(fun(106)) = fun(96) = fun(fun(107)) = fun(97) = ... = fun(101) = 91.

steps.title: Apply the else branch since 95 <= 100
steps.apply: Since 95 is not > 100, we go to the else branch: fun(95) = fun(fun(106)). Now evaluate the inner call: fun(106) = 106 - 10 = 96 (since 106 > 100). So fun(95) = fun(96).
steps.note: Each step of the unwinding increases the argument by (11-10)=1 until we exceed 100.

steps.title: Continue unwinding: fun(96) through fun(100)
steps.note: The pattern shows that for any x in [90,100], fun(x) = 91. This is the McCarthy 91 result.

solution.result: fun(95) = 91. The correct answer is C.

questions.formula_note: McCarthy 91 function: for x <= 100, M(x) = 91; for x > 100, M(x) = x - 10. The nested recursion fun(fun(x+11)) reduces to fun(x+1) when x+11 > 100, climbing by 1 each step until it reaches 101 where it returns 91.

questions.answer: C. Finds the middle number of a, b, c
questions.to_find: What does the function Trial(a, b, c) compute?

understand.plain: The function recursively rearranges the three arguments until it finds the one that is neither the largest nor the smallest — i.e., the median (middle) value. The base case returns b when a>=b and c<b, which means b is sandwiched between a and c, making b the median.

keywords.explain: The value that is neither the maximum nor the minimum among three numbers. For a, b, c the median m satisfies: the other two numbers are one >= m and one <= m.
When a >= b and c < b, we have a >= b > c, so b is the middle value. The function returns b.
keywords.example: Trial(7, 5, 3): a=7>=b=5 and c=3<b=5, so return 5 (the median)

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 300'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='500' height='300' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Trial(a, b, c) — Finding the Median</text><rect x='10' y='40' width='480' height='36' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='250' y='64' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Base Case: (a>=b) AND (c<b) → return b (b is median)</text><rect x='10' y='85' width='480' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Condition means: a >= b > c</text><text x='250' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>b is sandwiched between a (above) and c (below) → b is median</text><rect x='10' y='145' width='480' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='169' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>If (a>=b) but (c>=b): call Trial(a, c, b) — swap b and c</text><rect x='10' y='190' width='480' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='214' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>If (a<b): call Trial(b, a, c) — swap a and b to make a the larger</text><text x='250' y='255' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Example: Trial(3, 7, 5)</text><text x='250' y='278' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>→ Trial(7, 3, 5) → Trial(7, 5, 3) → return 5 (median)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Flowchart showing Trial(a,b,c): base case returns b when a>=b and c<b (meaning b is the median). Otherwise it recursively swaps arguments until b becomes the median.

given.aim: Determine what mathematical function Trial(a, b, c) computes

terms.meaning: Both a >= b and c < b hold, so b is between a and c: a >= b > c. This means b is the median.
terms.example: a=10, b=5, c=2: 10>=5 and 2<5, so return 5 which is median of {10,5,2}
terms.connects: This is the base case that identifies and returns the middle value

terms.term: Recursive call Trial(a, c, b) when a>=b but c>=b
terms.meaning: If a>=b but c is not less than b (so c>=b), then b may not be the median yet. We swap b and c in argument positions to re-examine.
terms.example: Trial(10, 3, 7): 10>=3 but 7>=3, so call Trial(10, 7, 3) — now 7 is in the b slot
terms.connects: Repositions c into the b slot so we can check if c is the median

terms.term: Recursive call Trial(b, a, c) when a<b
terms.meaning: If a < b, then b is larger than a. We promote b to the a-slot and put a in the b-slot to look for a smaller candidate for the median.
terms.example: Trial(3, 7, 5): 3<7 so call Trial(7, 3, 5) — 7 goes to a, 3 to b
terms.connects: Ensures the a-slot always holds a value >= b-slot to reach the base case

given.plan: Test with a concrete example to verify, then understand the invariant: the function rearranges {a,b,c} so that in the base case b is the median. Trace Trial(3,7,5) -> Trial(7,3,5) -> Trial(7,5,3) -> base: 7>=5 and 3<5 -> return 5 (median of 3,5,7).

steps.title: Analyze the base case
steps.formula_raw: if (a >= b) && (c < b) then return b
steps.apply: When the condition (a>=b) AND (c<b) holds, we have a >= b and b > c, i.e., a >= b > c. So b is strictly greater than c and less than or equal to a, making b the median (middle value) of {a, b, c}.
steps.note: The base case directly returns the median value b.

steps.title: Trace a concrete example: Trial(3, 7, 5)
steps.apply: Trial(3,7,5): a=3 < b=7, so call Trial(7,3,5). Now a=7>=b=3 but c=5>=b=3, so call Trial(7,5,3). Now a=7>=b=5 and c=3<b=5, so return b=5. The median of {3,5,7} is indeed 5.
steps.note: The recursion always terminates because argument rearrangement eventually satisfies the base case condition.

steps.title: Verify with another example: Trial(10, 2, 6)
steps.apply: Trial(10,2,6): a=10>=b=2 but c=6>=b=2, so call Trial(10,6,2). Now a=10>=b=6 and c=2<b=6, so return b=6. Median of {2,6,10} is 6. Correct.
steps.note: The function reliably finds the median, not the maximum or minimum.

solution.result: Trial(a, b, c) finds the middle number (median) of a, b, and c. The correct answer is C.

questions.formula_note: The function implements median-finding via recursive argument rearrangement: the invariant is that b is returned when a>=b>c, which precisely identifies b as the median of the three values.
